title: Saltfish fritters
subtitle: Jamaican crispy salt cod fritters
excerpt: Flaked desalinated salt cod folded into a thick batter of flour, egg, scotch bonnet, spring onion, and thyme, then fried until the outside is deeply crisp and the inside is soft with pockets of savoury fish. The batter is seasoned by the cod — no additional salt is needed if the fish is properly desalinated. Serve as a starter, a party snack, or alongside a Jamaican breakfast.
sourceNotes: Saltfish fritters (also called stamp and go) are one of the most recognisable Jamaican breakfast and snack foods. The name 'stamp and go' reflects their street-food origins — a quick, portable food. The recipe draws on the preserved salt cod tradition that was central to Jamaican cooking from the colonial period when dried salt cod was imported as a cheap protein for enslaved people.

recipe.batchNotes: Fry in multiple batches. The batter keeps for 2 hours.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: The saltfish must be soaked 24 to 48 hours before use. Change the water 3 to 4 times.

content.text: The salt cod must be properly soaked before use. Place it in a bowl of cold water in the fridge for 24 to 48 hours, changing the water 3 to 4 times. Before making the batter, taste a small flake of the boiled fish — it should be pleasantly savoury, not aggressively salty. If still too salty, continue soaking.

The batter should be thick enough to coat the back of a spoon heavily. Too thin and the fritters spread and go lacy; too thick and they are stodgy. If the batter is too thick after mixing, add a tablespoon of water. Fry at 170°C — any hotter and the outside burns before the centre is cooked.

After soaking the {{salt-cod}}, place it in fresh water, bring to a boil, and simmer for 10 minutes. Drain, cool, and flake into small pieces, removing any skin and bones. Combine the {{plain-flour}} and {{baking-powder}} in a bowl. Beat the {{eggs}} and {{whole-milk}} together, then add to the flour and whisk to a smooth thick batter. Fold in the flaked saltfish, {{scotch-bonnet}}, {{spring-onion}}, {{thyme}}, and {{black-pepper}}. Heat the {{vegetable-oil}} to 170°C. Drop tablespoons of batter into the oil and fry for 3 to 4 minutes, turning once, until deep golden all over. Drain on kitchen paper and serve hot.

items.fix: Always taste the boiled fish before adding to the batter. 48 hours with 4 water changes is the safe minimum for a strongly-salted piece

items.symptom: Fritters spreading flat in the oil
items.fix: Add a tablespoon of flour to the batter. It should fall from a spoon in a thick ribbon, not pour freely

items.symptom: Fritters dark outside and raw inside
items.fix: Drop the temperature to 165°C. Fritters cook in 3 to 4 minutes at the right temperature — rushing them burns the outside

content.text: Saltfish fritters are one of Jamaica's best-loved street snacks, known as stamp and go from their origins at Jamaican roadside vendors where speed was a selling point. They appear at the Jamaican breakfast table alongside ackee and saltfish, fried plantain, and hard-dough bread, but they are equally good as a party snack or starter. In the Jamaican diaspora in Britain they are a home-cooking staple for weekend mornings and gatherings, made from the same preserved salt cod used across Caribbean cooking.
